name: Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)
summary: A systematic method for identifying how a system can fail, assessing the severity and likelihood of each failure, and designing mitigations. In aerospace, where failure can mean loss of crew, FMEA is not optional — it is how you keep people alive.
whenToUseIt: Use FMEA during design, before testing, and after anomalies. Every critical component should have its failure modes mapped before it flies. This is especially important for human-rated systems, engines, and safety-critical avionics.
steps: Identify all components and their functions within the system
For each component, list every way it could fail (failure modes)
Determine the effect of each failure on the subsystem and overall mission
Rate severity (1-10), occurrence likelihood (1-10), and detectability (1-10)
Calculate Risk Priority Number (RPN = Severity x Occurrence x Detection)
Design mitigations for high-RPN items — redundancy, testing, design changes
Verify mitigations reduce RPN to acceptable levels
Review and update FMEA throughout the programme lifecycle
example: Merlin engine turbopump FMEA: Failure mode = 'turbine blade fracture'. Effect = 'loss of engine, potential vehicle failure'. Severity = 9. Occurrence = 2 (rare with proper metallurgy). Detection = 4 (vibration monitoring can catch precursors). RPN = 72. Mitigation: redundant engines (Falcon 9 can lose an engine and still complete mission), vibration health monitoring, regular turbopump inspection on reused boosters.
